title: Skeleton Crew and The Mist - The Ran(King) of King - Part 5
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IF966yryyqY

Daniel (@dungeondive) takes on two things at once in The Ran(King) of King: the 1985 collection Skeleton Crew, and — ranked separately, as he'll do with all King's novellas — The Mist. He reflects on how hard non-thematic short-story collections are to rate (best examples? worst? an average?), but values how this one, spanning 1968–1985, reads almost like a history of King finding his voice.

Skeleton Crew is a touch hit-and-miss but holds stone-cold classics — "The Jaunt" (his science-fiction foray, with one of King's most chilling final lines), "Survivor Type," "The Raft," "Word Processor of the Gods," and the creepy "The Monkey" — alongside some clunkers. The standout, though, is The Mist: he calls it incredible, immensely readable Lovecraftian horror that rejects purple prose for King's conversational tone, trapping a town in a supermarket against an outside force and an internal one. "Night of the Living Dead meets Tremors," he says — tight, cinematic, atmospheric — and praises Frank Darabont's faithful film and its bleaker ending (which King preferred).

On the board, Skeleton Crew lands mid-pack, but The Mist vaults to first — doing in a third of the pages what Desperation does.

----

Which Skeleton Crew story has stuck with you longest — and do you, like Daniel, rate The Mist among King's very best?
